news_summary: Recent discoveries about alien planet rotations provide new insights into planetary formation, while evidence continues to support dark energy as the driving force behind the universe's accelerating expansion.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes découvertes concernant la rotation des planètes extraterrestres apportent un nouvel éclairage sur la formation des planètes, tandis que les données continuent de confirmer que l'énergie noire est le moteur de l'expansion accélérée de l'univers.
news_summary_es: Los recientes descubrimientos sobre la rotación de los planetas extra-solares aportan nuevos conocimientos sobre la formación planetaria, mientras que las pruebas siguen apuntando a que la energía oscura es la fuerza motriz que impulsa la expansión acelerada del universo.

articles.title: Alien planet spins revealed a hidden clue to how worlds form
articles.summary: Using the Keck Observatory, astronomers measured the spins of dozens of giant planets and brown dwarfs orbiting distant stars. They found that giant planets can spin faster than much more massive brown dwarfs, challenging simple assumptions about mass and rotation. The results suggest that magnetic fields and formation processes play a major role in determining how fast worlds end up spinning.
articles.content:
Astronomers have long suspected that a planet's mass and its rotation speed are linked. In our own Solar System, Jupiter and Saturn provide striking examples. Despite their enormous size, both complete a full rotation in about 10 hours and account for a large share of the Solar System's total rotational energy.

To test whether this relationship extends beyond our cosmic neighborhood, researchers used the W. M. Keck Observatory on Maunakea, Hawai'i, to study a large sample of distant giant worlds. Their survey included 32 gas giants and brown dwarf companions in other star systems, including 6 planets larger than Jupiter and 25 brown dwarf companions.

The observations revealed an intriguing trend. When factors such as mass, size, and age are considered, giant gas planets tend to rotate faster than more massive brown dwarfs. To strengthen their analysis, the researchers also incorporated previous spin measurements from other studies, creating a carefully selected dataset that included 43 stellar/substellar companions and giant planets, along with 54 free-floating brown dwarfs and planetary-mass objects.

The international team was led by scientists at Northwestern University's Center for Interdisciplinary Exploration and Research in Astrophysics (CIERA). Collaborators included researchers from the Center for Astrophysics and Space Sciences (CASS) at UC San Diego, the Division of Geological & Planetary Sciences (GPS) at Caltech, the W. M. Keck Observatory, the Steward Observatory, the James C. Wyant College of Optical Sciences, NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory, and several other institutions. Their findings were published in The Astronomical Journal.

Measuring the Spin of Distant Worlds

Many of the planets examined orbit their stars at distances ranging from tens to hundreds of Astronomical Units (AUs), the distance between Earth and the Sun. Scientists are still trying to determine how these faraway worlds form. Some may gradually emerge within disks of gas and dust surrounding young stars, while others could form through a process more similar to the collapse that creates stars themselves.

To investigate, the researchers used the Keck Planet Imager and Characterizer (KPIC), a specialized instrument capable of isolating light coming directly from these distant worlds. As a planet rotates, features in its atmosphere cause subtle broadening in its spectrum. By measuring these changes, astronomers can determine how quickly the object is spinning.

Lead author Dino Chih-Chun Hsu, a researcher at CIERA, explained the significance of these measurements in a W. M. Keck Observatory press release:

"Spin is a fossil record of how a planet formed. By measuring how quickly these worlds rotate, we can start to piece together the physical processes that shaped them tens to hundreds of millions of years ago. With KPIC, we can detect these tiny signals that reveal a planet's rotation around other nearby stars. Our results suggest that both the planet's mass and the ratio between the planet's mass and its star's mass influence how fast the planet ultimately spins. That helps us narrow down the physics of how these systems form."

A Giant Planet Outspins a Much Larger Neighbor

One of the clearest examples comes from the HR 8799 system. There, a gas giant about 7 times the mass of Jupiter rotates six times faster than a brown dwarf companion that is roughly 24 times Jupiter's mass.

Researchers believe the difference may be linked to magnetic interactions early in the objects' histories. A stronger magnetic field can interact more intensely with the surrounding circumplanetary disk, slowing rotation over time. In this case, the more massive brown dwarf likely lost more of its original spin because of its stronger magnetic field.

The findings are helping scientists better understand not only distant planetary systems but also the origins of our own Solar System. Hsu said:

"The way that angular momentum is distributed among planets influences the overall architecture of a planetary system. Even Earth's rotation and magnetic field ultimately connect to how that spin budget was divided when the solar system formed. KPIC is the first instrument of its kind, opening an entirely new way to study exoplanets. It allowed us to measure properties like spin that were previously almost impossible to detect."

Future Studies of Rogue Planets and Exoplanet Atmospheres

The team plans to expand this work by studying the rotation of free-floating planets (FFPs), often called "Rogue Planets." Researchers also hope to investigate the chemical makeup of these worlds' atmospheres.

Future observations will benefit from new technology, including the Keck Observatory's upcoming HISPEC (High-resolution Infrared Spectrograph for Exoplanet Characterization), scheduled to begin operations in 2027. According to Hsu, the new instrument will make it possible to study smaller and more distant worlds than ever before.

Jason Wang, an Assistant Professor at Northwestern University and a co-author of the study, said:

"We took the lessons learned from KPIC, and put them into HISPEC, which will have better sensitivity, higher spectral resolution, and wider wavelength coverage. With HISPEC we will be able to drastically increase the number of planets that we can measure spins of, and in particular, we can study planets closer to our own Jupiter in nature to see if our own Jupiter is typical."

Researchers believe they are only beginning to uncover what planetary rotation can reveal.

"We're just beginning to explore what planetary spin can tell us," said Hsu. "With future instruments and larger telescopes, we'll be able to measure spins for even more worlds and connect rotation, chemistry, and formation history across entire planetary systems."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about planetary spin and formation processes using advanced instruments, providing new insights into how planetary systems form. This has broad implications for understanding our own Solar System and exoplanetary systems, representing a meaningful breakthrough in astrophysics with tangible benefits for scientific knowledge.
articles.personality_title: Astronomers uncover new clues about how planets spin and form
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wondered how a planet’s size and mass affect how fast it spins. In our Solar System, big planets like Jupiter and Saturn spin quickly, but it was unclear if this is true for planets around other stars.

**What happened** – Using a special instrument called KPIC at the Keck Observatory in Hawaii, astronomers measured the spin speeds of 32 giant planets and brown dwarfs orbiting distant stars. They found that giant planets spin faster than brown dwarfs, even when the brown dwarfs are much more massive. The team studied how factors like mass, size, and age affect spin, and combined their data with earlier studies to get a clearer picture.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that a planet’s spin is influenced not just by its size but also by magnetic fields and how it formed. For example, in the HR 8799 system, a planet seven times Jupiter’s mass spins six times faster than a bigger brown dwarf. This helps scientists understand that magnetic forces can slow down rotation. These insights improve our knowledge of how planets and their systems develop, including our own Solar System.

**What’s next step** – Researchers plan to study the spins of “rogue planets” that float freely in space without a star. They will also use new instruments like HISPEC, which starts in 2027, to measure spins of smaller and more distant planets. This will help scientists learn more about a planet’s atmosphere and formation.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Measuring how fast planets spin reveals important details about their origins and the forces shaping planetary systems across the galaxy.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les astronomes découvrent de nouveaux indices sur la rotation et la formation des planètes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques se sont longtemps demandé comment la taille et la masse d’une planète influencent sa vitesse de rotation. Dans notre système solaire, les grandes planètes comme Jupiter et Saturne tournent rapidement, mais on ne savait pas si c’était pareil pour les planètes autour d’autres étoiles.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Grâce à un instrument spécial appelé KPIC à l’Observatoire Keck à Hawaï, des astronomes ont mesuré la vitesse de rotation de 32 géantes gazeuses et naines brunes en orbite autour d’étoiles lointaines. Ils ont découvert que les planètes géantes tournent plus vite que les naines brunes, même si ces dernières sont beaucoup plus massives. L’équipe a étudié l’effet de la masse, de la taille et de l’âge sur la rotation et a combiné ses données avec des études précédentes pour mieux comprendre.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que la vitesse de rotation d’une planète dépend non seulement de sa taille, mais aussi des champs magnétiques et de sa façon de se former. Par exemple, dans le système HR 8799, une planète sept fois plus massive que Jupiter tourne six fois plus vite qu’une naine brune plus grosse. Cela aide les scientifiques à comprendre que les forces magnétiques peuvent ralentir la rotation. Ces résultats améliorent notre connaissance de la formation des planètes et de leurs systèmes, y compris le nôtre.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont étudier la rotation des « planètes errantes » qui flottent dans l’espace sans étoile. Ils utiliseront aussi de nouveaux instruments comme HISPEC, qui commencera en 2027, pour mesurer la rotation de planètes plus petites et plus éloignées. Cela les aidera à en savoir plus sur l’atmosphère et la formation des planètes.

**Une phrase clé** – Mesurer la vitesse de rotation des planètes révèle des détails importants sur leur origine et les forces qui façonnent les systèmes planétaires dans la galaxie.
articles.personality_title_es: Astrónomos descubren nuevas pistas sobre cómo giran y se forman los planetas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han querido saber durante mucho tiempo cómo afectan el tamaño y la masa de un planeta a la velocidad con que gira. En nuestro Sistema Solar, planetas grandes como Júpiter y Saturno giran rápido, pero no estaba claro si esto ocurre igual en planetas de otras estrellas.

**Qué pasó** – Usando un instrumento especial llamado KPIC en el Observatorio Keck en Hawái, astrónomos midieron la velocidad de giro de 32 planetas gigantes y enanas marrones que orbitan estrellas lejanas. Descubrieron que los planetas gigantes giran más rápido que las enanas marrones, aunque estas sean mucho más masivas. El equipo analizó cómo la masa, tamaño y edad afectan la rotación y combinó sus datos con estudios anteriores para entender mejor.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo muestra que la velocidad de giro de un planeta depende no solo de su tamaño, sino también de los campos magnéticos y cómo se formó. Por ejemplo, en el sistema HR 8799, un planeta siete veces más masivo que Júpiter gira seis veces más rápido que una enana marrón mayor. Esto ayuda a los científicos a entender que las fuerzas magnéticas pueden frenar la rotación. Estos resultados mejoran nuestro conocimiento sobre cómo se forman los planetas y sus sistemas, incluido el nuestro.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean estudiar la rotación de “planetas errantes” que flotan libres en el espacio sin una estrella. También usarán nuevos instrumentos como HISPEC, que empezará a funcionar en 2027, para medir la rotación de planetas más pequeños y lejanos. Esto les ayudará a aprender más sobre la atmósfera y la formación de los planetas.

**Una frase clave** – Medir la velocidad de giro de los planetas revela detalles importantes sobre su origen y las fuerzas que moldean los sistemas planetarios en toda la galaxia.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two swirling celestial orbs—a smaller, brightly glowing gas giant spinning rapidly with visible atmospheric bands, and a larger, dimmer brown dwarf rotating more slowly—set against a softly glowing starry background with subtle rings of dust and light representing circumstellar disks, rendered in natural earth tones and gentle blues.

articles.title: Dark energy survives major challenge as universe keeps accelerating
articles.summary: A bold claim that the universe’s accelerating expansion was an illusion has been put to the test—and failed. Researchers found that the study behind the controversy made key mistakes when analyzing supernova data. After revisiting the evidence, astronomers concluded that cosmic acceleration remains as strong as ever.
articles.content:
Astronomers say a recent challenge to one of the most important discoveries in modern cosmology has been resolved, with new research confirming that the universe is still expanding at an accelerating rate.

The debate began in late 2025 when a group of astronomers published findings suggesting that evidence for dark energy, the mysterious phenomenon thought to drive the universe's accelerating expansion, was weakening. Their analysis raised the possibility that the expansion of the universe might no longer be speeding up.

The researchers argued that the standard approach for measuring cosmic expansion using supernovae, the explosive deaths of stars, contained fundamental flaws.

However, a new investigation led by the University of Southampton has reexamined the data and reached a different conclusion. According to the team, the universe continues to behave exactly as current cosmological models predict.

The study, published in the Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society, includes Nobel Prize-winning astrophysicists Professor Adam Riess and Professor Brian Schmidt.

New Analysis Supports Cosmic Acceleration

Lead author Dr. Phil Wiseman of the University of Southampton said the controversy that emerged after the 2025 claims stemmed from a misunderstanding of the data rather than a problem with the universe itself.

He added: "The previous and well accepted measurements were, in fact, fine and our current understanding of the fate of the universe remains robust.

"Thankfully we have averted this crisis, but the mystery about why the universe is still accelerating in size remains.

"By proving our measurements are correct, we can get back to trying to understand what dark energy actually is, rather than wondering if it exists at all."

The discovery that the universe's expansion is accelerating was originally made by Riess, Schmidt, and American astrophysicist Saul Perlmutter. Their groundbreaking work earned the trio the 2011 Nobel Prize in Physics.

Had the 2025 claims proven correct, they would have overturned that landmark discovery and called into question nearly 30 years of cosmological research.

Professor Adam Riess said: "Extraordinary claims require especially careful testing.

"What we find is that when we calibrate these supernovae, accounting for different host environments and populations, the evidence for cosmic acceleration remains remarkably consistent."

Supernova Measurements Reexamined

To investigate the issue, the Southampton team focused on Type Ia supernovae, extremely bright explosions of white dwarf stars that are used as cosmic distance markers across the universe.

The 2025 study argued that the peak brightness of these supernovae changes as the universe ages. If true, that effect could make astronomers mistakenly conclude that the universe is accelerating when it is actually slowing down.

The new analysis found that the problem was not with the supernovae themselves, but with how their ages had been estimated.

According to the researchers, the earlier study incorrectly treated the age of a galaxy as being the same as the age of the star that eventually exploded as a supernova.

The team also reported that the 2025 analysis did not properly account for the mass of host galaxies, a standard correction routinely used in modern cosmology to improve measurement accuracy.

Lessons for Understanding Dark Energy

Professor Mark Sullivan of the University of Southampton emphasized that questioning accepted ideas is an essential part of scientific progress.

He added: "This is how progress is made. Although this idea did not turn out correct, it has opened up new ways of thinking about how supernovae explode and how we can measure dark energy more accurately."

Co-author Dr. Brodie Popovic said the project provided an opportunity to revisit assumptions that underpin modern cosmology.

He added: "We've recently been really focused on astrophysics of the explosions and how they impact cosmology.

"This was a good opportunity to go back and go over all of our assumptions -- it turns out, yes, we do understand this stuff and we're accounting for it in our cosmology measurement."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific confirmation that the universe's accelerating expansion, driven by dark energy, remains valid after a major challenge. This has broad implications for cosmology and our understanding of the universe, representing a meaningful breakthrough in space science with detailed context and expert involvement.
articles.personality_title: New study confirms universe is still expanding faster and faster
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In 2025, some astronomers suggested that the universe might not be speeding up in its expansion as we thought. They questioned the evidence for dark energy, a mysterious force believed to push the universe to grow faster over time. This idea challenged a major discovery made nearly 30 years ago.

**What happened** – A team from the University of Southampton reexamined the data used to measure the universe’s expansion. They focused on supernovae, which are exploding stars used as markers to measure distances in space. The new study found mistakes in how the earlier group analyzed the brightness and ages of these supernovae and their galaxies. After correcting these errors, the researchers confirmed that the universe is still accelerating in its growth.

**Impact** – This result supports the original discovery that won the 2011 Nobel Prize for showing the universe’s expansion is speeding up. It means that dark energy remains the best explanation for this acceleration. The new study also improves how scientists measure cosmic distances by better understanding supernova explosions and their environments.

**What's next step** – With this confirmation, astronomers can focus again on learning more about what dark energy really is and how it works. They will continue studying supernovae and other cosmic clues to refine their measurements and theories about the universe’s future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Careful reanalysis shows the universe’s expansion is still accelerating, confirming dark energy’s key role in shaping cosmic growth.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle étude confirme que l’univers continue de s’étendre de plus en plus vite
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En 2025, certains astronomes ont suggéré que l’univers ne s’accélérait peut-être plus dans son expansion, contrairement à ce que l’on pensait. Ils ont remis en question les preuves de l’énergie noire, une force mystérieuse censée pousser l’univers à grandir plus vite avec le temps. Cette idée remettait en cause une grande découverte faite il y a presque 30 ans.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de l’Université de Southampton a réexaminé les données utilisées pour mesurer l’expansion de l’univers. Ils se sont concentrés sur les supernovae, des étoiles en explosion utilisées comme repères pour mesurer les distances dans l’espace. La nouvelle étude a trouvé des erreurs dans la façon dont le groupe précédent avait analysé la luminosité et l’âge de ces supernovae et de leurs galaxies. Après correction, les chercheurs ont confirmé que l’univers continue bien de s’étendre de plus en plus vite.

**Impact** – Ce résultat soutient la découverte originale qui a valu le prix Nobel en 2011, montrant que l’expansion de l’univers s’accélère. Cela signifie que l’énergie noire reste la meilleure explication pour cette accélération. La nouvelle étude améliore aussi la façon dont les scientifiques mesurent les distances cosmiques en comprenant mieux les explosions de supernovae et leur environnement.

**Prochaine étape** – Avec cette confirmation, les astronomes peuvent se concentrer à nouveau sur la compréhension de ce qu’est vraiment l’énergie noire et comment elle fonctionne. Ils continueront d’étudier les supernovae et d’autres indices cosmiques pour affiner leurs mesures et leurs théories sur l’avenir de l’univers.

**Phrase clé** – Une réanalyse minutieuse montre que l’expansion de l’univers s’accélère toujours, confirmant le rôle clé de l’énergie noire dans la croissance cosmique.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo estudio confirma que el universo sigue expandiéndose cada vez más rápido
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En 2025, algunos astrónomos sugirieron que el universo podría no estar acelerando su expansión como se creía. Cuestionaron la evidencia de la energía oscura, una fuerza misteriosa que se piensa impulsa al universo a crecer más rápido con el tiempo. Esta idea desafiaba un gran descubrimiento hecho hace casi 30 años.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de la Universidad de Southampton revisó los datos usados para medir la expansión del universo. Se centraron en supernovas, explosiones de estrellas que se usan como marcadores para medir distancias en el espacio. El nuevo estudio encontró errores en cómo el grupo anterior analizó el brillo y la edad de estas supernovas y sus galaxias. Tras corregirlos, los investigadores confirmaron que el universo sigue acelerando su crecimiento.

**Impacto** – Este resultado apoya el descubrimiento original que ganó el Premio Nobel en 2011, mostrando que la expansión del universo está acelerándose. Esto significa que la energía oscura sigue siendo la mejor explicación para esta aceleración. El nuevo estudio también mejora cómo los científicos miden las distancias cósmicas al entender mejor las explosiones de supernovas y sus entornos.

**Próximo paso** – Con esta confirmación, los astrónomos pueden enfocarse otra vez en aprender más sobre qué es realmente la energía oscura y cómo funciona. Seguirán estudiando supernovas y otras pistas cósmicas para perfeccionar sus mediciones y teorías sobre el futuro del universo.

**Frase clave** – Un nuevo análisis cuidadoso muestra que la expansión del universo sigue acelerándose, confirmando el papel clave de la energía oscura en el crecimiento cósmico.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vast cosmic landscape featuring glowing, stylized Type Ia supernovae bursting brightly across a gently expanding universe, with subtle swirling patterns symbolizing dark energy gently pushing galaxies apart, all rendered in soft, natural hues of deep blues, purples, and warm golds, evoking a sense of ongoing discovery and cosmic harmony.
